Article.ArticleTitle: Quantifying pathogen exposure variability from surface contacts with force-dependent transfer models.

Abstract.AbstractText: Transfer rate is critical in fomite transmission and highly dependent on surface material and touch force. However, the wide variability of touch force across individuals and surfaces is often overlooked, limiting the accuracy of existing models. In this study, we developed a force-dependent transfer model combining lab measurements with behavioral data from office surfaces. Transfer rates were experimentally quantified across multiple surface materials under varying force levels. Touch force was collected across 15 surfaces and 2584 surface contacts were recording via computer vision (93.4 % accuracy) in an office environment. Pathogen simulations comparing variable and fixed transfer rates showed exposure differences ranging from 86.0 % to 207.7 %, revealing significant underestimation or overestimation when force variability is ignored. These findings highlight the physiological relevance of incorporating touch force into transfer modeling and underscore its importance in accurately capturing exposure heterogeneity in fomite transmission.
